Name: spectral_jacobian_routing
Title: Certified Latency-Aware Routing via Spectral Jacobian Proxies: A PAC-Bayes Framework for Test-Time Layer Selection
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spectral properties of layer-wise Jacobians encode sufficient information to enable theoretically-certified, training-free routing decisions at inference time, allowing dynamic layer skipping with provable accuracy-latency tradeoffs bounded by PAC-Bayes stability margins. This addresses a critical gap: existing early-exit and dynamic-depth methods either lack theoretical guarantees or require expensive retraining. By anchoring routing in spectral stability rather than learned confidence scores, we decouple routing from task-specific training, enabling deployment-time adaptation without model modification. The key innovation is formalizing how spectral Lipschitz bounds on layer-wise perturbations translate into end-to-end accuracy guarantees, enabling online routing with rigorously bounded computational overhead.
Related Work: Early-exit networks (Huang et al., 2018; Teerapittayanon et al., 2016) and dynamic-depth inference (Bolukbasi et al., 2017) enable variable computation but rely on learned classifiers or confidence scores, requiring task-specific training and providing no formal accuracy guarantees. Spectral analysis of neural networks (Bartlett et al., 2017; Neyshabur et al., 2015) establishes generalization bounds via operator norms but focuses on training-time regularization, not inference-time routing. PAC-Bayes frameworks (McAllester, 1999; Germain et al., 2016) provide certified bounds but are typically applied post-hoc to fixed models. Test-time optimization (Ramalho & Garnelo, 2019; Nichol et al., 2018) explores implicit optimization but assumes differentiable test-time objectives, not discrete routing. Our work uniquely combines spectral Jacobian analysis with PAC-Bayes bounds to enable training-free, certified routing. Unlike confidence-based early exits, we derive explicit perturbation budgets from layer-wise spectral norms. Unlike post-hoc PAC-Bayes analysis, we embed stability certification directly into the routing protocol, enabling online, amortized overhead accounting.
Abstract: Inference latency and accuracy are fundamentally coupled in deep networks: skipping layers reduces computation but risks accuracy loss. Existing dynamic-depth methods lack theoretical guarantees or require expensive retraining. We propose a novel framework for certified latency-aware routing by directly leveraging spectral properties of layer-wise Jacobians. Our key insight is that the spectral norm of each layer's Jacobian provides a tight, training-free proxy for perturbation propagation: skipping a layer induces activation perturbations whose magnitude is bounded by the cumulative spectral norm along the path. We formalize this via a PAC-Bayes framework, deriving explicit stability margins that guarantee end-to-end accuracy loss remains below a user-specified threshold for any routing path. Our algorithm, SpectralRouter, makes online routing decisions via a single forward pass computing layer-wise Jacobian singular values (amortized into wall-clock budgets via randomized SVD), then selects skip/execute decisions to maximize throughput subject to accuracy constraints. We establish that spectral routing decisions are robust to input perturbations and provide dimension-free generalization bounds. Experiments on ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and adversarial domains demonstrate that SpectralRouter achieves Pareto-optimal accuracy-latency tradeoffs, matching or exceeding confidence-based early exits while incurring only 2-4% computational overhead for spectral estimation. Critically, routing decisions transfer across datasets and model variants without retraining, enabling plug-and-play deployment.

Experiments.name: Spectral Jacobian Computation and Overhead Accounting
Experiments.description: For ResNet-50, VGG-16, and MobileNetV2, compute layer-wise Jacobian spectral norms using power iteration (k=1-3 steps) and randomized SVD (rank-10 approximation). Measure wall-clock time for spectral estimation as percentage of forward pass. Use GPU profiling (NVIDIA Nsys) to decompose overhead into: (1) Jacobian-vector products, (2) SVD computation, (3) routing decision logic. Quantify amortization: run 100 inference samples and measure amortized overhead per sample. Target: <4% overhead vs. baseline forward pass.

Experiments.name: Certified Accuracy-Latency Tradeoffs
Experiments.description: On ImageNet validation (50K samples), implement SpectralRouter with user-specified accuracy loss budgets (0%, 1%, 2%, 5%). For each budget, compute per-layer skip thresholds derived from PAC-Bayes bounds: for layer i, skip if cumulative spectral norm ≤ τ(δ), where τ encodes the remaining accuracy budget and PAC-Bayes margin. Measure: (1) actual accuracy loss vs. predicted bound, (2) latency reduction (measured via GPU wall-clock time), (3) Pareto frontier vs. baseline early-exit methods. Compare against: (a) confidence-based early exits (trained on ImageNet), (b) random layer skipping, (c) oracle skipping (computed offline via full validation).

Experiments.name: Robustness to Input Perturbations and Domain Shift
Experiments.description: Test routing stability under structured perturbations: (1) Gaussian noise (σ ∈ [0.01, 0.1]), (2) adversarial perturbations (FGSM, ε ∈ [0.01, 0.3]), (3) domain shift (CIFAR-100 → ImageNet-C, Stylized ImageNet). For each perturbation type, measure: (1) routing decision consistency (% samples with same routing path as clean input), (2) accuracy loss correlation between clean and perturbed inputs, (3) spectral norm stability (relative change in layer-wise singular values). Hypothesis: routing decisions should be stable (>90% consistency) under small perturbations, with accuracy loss gracefully degrading.

Experiments.name: Transfer of Routing Policies Across Model Variants
Experiments.description: Train routing policies on ResNet-50 (ImageNet), then apply to: (1) ResNet-101, (2) ResNet-50 with different batch norms, (3) EfficientNet-B0, (4) Vision Transformer-Base. For each target model, measure: (1) accuracy when using source-model routing thresholds, (2) latency reduction, (3) how much retuning is needed (how many validation samples required to recompute τ thresholds). Compare against: (a) retraining early-exit classifiers from scratch, (b) confidence-based routing trained on source model.

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Bound Tightness and Generalization
Experiments.description: Derive explicit PAC-Bayes bounds for SpectralRouter: for a given routing policy, compute the bound as a function of layer-wise spectral norms, model complexity (operator norm, Frobenius norm), and sample size. Test on held-out test set (10K ImageNet samples) and measure: (1) empirical accuracy loss, (2) predicted PAC-Bayes bound, (3) bound slack (ratio of bound to empirical loss). Vary model size (ResNet-18/50/101) and accuracy budget (1%-5%) to characterize how bounds scale. Hypothesis: bounds should hold with high probability (>95%) and slack should be <10x for realistic settings.

Experiments.name: Comparison with Confidence-Based and Learned Early-Exit Methods
Experiments.description: Benchmark against: (1) B-Lites (Bolukbasi et al., 2017), (2) DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2021), (3) FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020). For each method, measure on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-100: (1) accuracy-latency Pareto frontier, (2) training time (if applicable), (3) transferability to new datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet-21K), (4) robustness to adversarial perturbations. SpectralRouter should achieve comparable or better Pareto frontiers while requiring zero retraining.
metrics: Adversarial robustness (% accuracy drop at ε=0.1)

Experiments.name: Mechanistic Analysis: Spectral Norm Predictiveness
Experiments.description: For each layer in ResNet-50, compute: (1) layer-wise spectral norm σ_i, (2) empirical perturbation propagation (via finite differences: ||f(x + δ) - f(x)|| / ||δ|| for small random δ), (3) correlation between σ_i and actual perturbation amplification. Plot scatter: predicted vs. empirical perturbation propagation. Hypothesis: spectral norms should tightly predict actual perturbation amplification (R² > 0.9). Identify layers where prediction fails and characterize (e.g., ReLU saturation, skip connections).
metrics: Correlation R² (spectral norm vs. empirical perturbation)

Experiments.name: Ablation: Impact of SVD Rank and Power Iteration Steps
Experiments.description: For SpectralRouter, vary: (1) SVD rank k ∈ [1, 5, 10, 20], (2) power iteration steps p ∈ [1, 2, 3, 5]. For each configuration, measure: (1) accuracy-latency tradeoff, (2) computational overhead, (3) bound tightness. Hypothesis: k=10, p=2 should provide good balance (tight bounds, <4% overhead). Quantify diminishing returns of higher rank/iterations.

Experiments.name: Single-GPU Feasibility and Memory Efficiency
Experiments.description: Implement SpectralRouter on single GPU (NVIDIA A100 80GB, V100 32GB). Measure: (1) peak memory usage during inference (spectral estimation + forward pass), (2) throughput (samples/sec), (3) whether spectral computation can be pipelined with forward pass to avoid memory spikes. Compare memory footprint vs. batch-wise early exits (which require storing intermediate activations for exit classifiers).

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Spectral Norm Underestimation via Randomized SVD
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Power iteration and randomized SVD with limited rank/iterations may underestimate true spectral norms, leading to overly aggressive layer skipping and accuracy degradation. Mitigation: (1) Provide rigorous concentration bounds for randomized SVD error, (2) Use oversampling in SVD (rank k + buffer), (3) Validate spectral estimates on small sample of validation data and adjust thresholds adaptively.

PAC-Bayes bounds are often loose (10-100x slack) in practice, potentially leading to overly conservative routing decisions and minimal latency gains. Mitigation: (1) Empirically characterize bound tightness on multiple architectures, (2) Develop tighter bounds using recent advances (e.g., catoni bounds), (3) Use empirical Bernstein bounds calibrated on held-out validation set.

Computing Jacobian-vector products and SVD, even with amortization, may exceed 4% overhead budget on some architectures (e.g., MobileNets with small layers). Mitigation: (1) Use cheaper spectral proxies (e.g., layer-wise norms of weight matrices as lower bound), (2) Cache spectral estimates across batches, (3) Implement GPU kernels for optimized Jacobian-vector products.

Spectral properties may be architecture-specific; routing policies learned on ResNet may not transfer to Vision Transformers or other fundamentally different architectures. Mitigation: (1) Empirically test transfer across diverse architectures (CNNs, ViTs, RNNs), (2) Develop architecture-agnostic spectral proxies, (3) Provide framework for quick retuning on new architectures (few validation samples).

Adversarial perturbations may exploit spectral properties; an attacker could craft inputs with large Jacobians to trigger conservative routing and reduce throughput. Mitigation: (1) Analyze adversarial robustness formally (certified defenses), (2) Test on standard adversarial benchmarks (FGSM, PGD), (3) Compare robustness vs. confidence-based methods.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Skip Connections and Residual Networks Complicate Analysis
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Skip connections in ResNets allow information to bypass layers, violating the assumption that perturbations propagate monotonically. Spectral analysis must account for alternative paths. Mitigation: (1) Extend framework to compute effective spectral norms over all paths, (2) Empirically validate on ResNets (most widely used architecture), (3) Provide separate analysis for architectures with/without skip connections.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Limited Applicability to Recurrent and Transformer Architectures
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Spectral routing assumes feedforward computation; recurrent networks and transformers with attention have fundamentally different information flow. Mitigation: (1) Focus initial work on CNNs (ResNets, EfficientNets), (2) Develop separate framework for transformers (spectral analysis of attention matrices), (3) Leave RNNs as future work.

Spectral estimation involves multiple hyperparameters (SVD rank, power iteration steps, threshold selection). Sensitivity analysis could reveal brittle design. Mitigation: (1) Conduct thorough ablation studies, (2) Provide exact hyperparameter settings for all experiments, (3) Release open-source code with deterministic seeding, (4) Report variance across 10+ random seeds.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Validation Set Requirement for Threshold Tuning
Risk Factors and Limitations.description: Deriving routing thresholds from PAC-Bayes bounds requires some labeled validation data. In zero-shot settings (no validation data), thresholds must be set conservatively, reducing latency gains. Mitigation: (1) Provide default conservative thresholds, (2) Show that even default thresholds achieve reasonable speedups, (3) Develop unsupervised threshold selection (e.g., based on activation statistics).
